claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

To help determine your eligibility, I have a few more questions:

Are you responsible for Alex's care, and does he live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't live with me at the moment. He lives with his mum. But I do contribute towards his upkeep - I help out with things like clothes, shoes, and other bits he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. Since Alex lives with his mum rather than with you, I need to ask a couple more follow-up questions to understand the situation better.

Is Alex's mum already claiming Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

Even though you contribute financially towards Alex's needs, to be eligible for Child Benefit, you generally need to be the person the child lives with and who is responsible for their day-to-day care.

Could you tell me why Alex's mum isn't claiming Child Benefit for him? Is there a particular reason she hasn't applied, or would she be willing for you to claim instead?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: She just hasn't applied for it. I'm the one who's been helping out financially with his needs, so I thought I should be the one to claim it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I appreciate you explaining your situation. Let me just check the specific rules around this to make sure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience while I checked the rules.

Based on the information you've provided, here's what I can tell you:

To qualify for Child Benefit, you normally need to either:
1. **Live with the child**, OR
2. **Be paying at least the same amount as the Child Benefit rate** (or equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes, or pocket money

Since Alex doesn't live with you but you are contributing towards his upkeep (clothes, shoes, and other necessities), you may still be eligible **if** your contributions are at least equal to the Child Benefit amount.

To help me determine this, could you tell me roughly how much you contribute towards Alex's needs each week or month? For reference, the Child Benefit rate is currently around £25.60 per week for a first child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £30 a week towards his upkeep, so that should cover it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's very helpful.

Since you're contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit rate (approximately £25.60 per week for a first child), you could potentially qualify for Child Benefit based on your financial contributions.

I just have a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually - I live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's an important detail.

Living abroad can affect your eligibility for Child Benefit. To help me understand your situation better:

Are you a Crown servant (for example, working for the UK government or armed forces overseas), or do you work in a country within the European Economic Area (EEA) or Switzerland?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or working in the EEA or Switzerland. I'm just living and working abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Based on all the information you've provided, I now have enough to make an assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you would not be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**Here's why:**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you must normally **live in the UK**. While there are some exceptions for people living abroad (such as Crown servants or those working in the EEA or Switzerland), you've confirmed that none of these apply to your situation.

Although your financial contributions of £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep do exceed the Child Benefit rate, this alone isn't enough to qualify when you don't meet the residency requirement.

**What this means for Alex:**

The good news is that Alex's mum, who lives with him, would likely be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him. Since she hasn't applied yet, she may want to consider doing so, as only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child and she meets the key criteria of living with Alex and being responsible for his day-to-day care.

I hope this helps clarify the situation. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: To qualify for Child Benefit, a person must normally live in the UK. While there are exceptions for Crown servants and those working in certain countries (EEA/Switzerland), the claimant has confirmed they do not fall into any of these categories. Although the claimant does contribute £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit rate), this alone is not sufficient to qualify when the claimant lives abroad and does not meet any of the exceptions for those living outside the UK. The basic eligibility criteria states you must 'live in the UK' to qualify for Child Benefit, and since the claimant lives abroad without meeting any of the permitted exceptions, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. While they do contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep at a level that exceeds the Child Benefit rate (£30 per week vs approximately £25.60), the claimant does not meet the fundamental requirement of living in the UK. They are not a Crown servant and do not work in the EEA or Switzerland, so none of the exceptions for those living abroad apply. Alex's mother, who lives with Alex in the UK, would likely be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit.

The basic eligibility criteria states you must 'live in the UK' to qualify for Child Benefit, and since the claimant lives abroad without meeting any of the permitted exceptions, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

They are not a Crown servant and do not work in the EEA or Switzerland, so none of the exceptions for those living abroad apply. Alex's mother, who lives with Alex in the UK, would likely be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit.
